title: Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
text:
1.1 Introduction
Diagnostic tests in medicine and biology rarely produce a simple yes/no result. More often, they produce a continuous measurement, for example a biomarker concentration, an imaging score, a blood pressure reading. The researcher must decide how well that measurement distinguishes between individuals with and without a condition. Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves are one of the most widely used tools for evaluating this kind of diagnostic performance.
This guide walks through the full ROC analysis workflow in R: deciding whether ROC analysis is appropriate for your data, preparing your dataset, generating and visualizing the ROC curve, and computing and interpreting the Area Under the Curve (AUC). All steps are demonstrated with real datasets and annotated code using the pROC package (Robin et al. 2011).
This guide does not cover advanced topics such as covariate-adjusted ROC curves, time-dependent ROC analysis, or optimal threshold selection (e.g., the Youden Index). Those topics are excluded to keep the focus on foundational ROC analysis.
crumbs: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R

title: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
text:
1.2 Prerequisites

1.2.1 Knowledge
You should be familiar with:

Sensitivity: the probability that the test is positive given the person has the disease (true positive rate)
Specificity: the probability that the test is negative given the person does not have the disease (true negative rate)
Binary outcomes: your reference standard should classify each individual as diseased (1) or non-diseased (0)

No prior experience with ROC curves is needed


1.2.2 Software
You will need R and RStudio installed, along with the following packages:

#loading the following packages and install if necessary
library(pROC)

Warning: package 'pROC' was built under R version 4.4.3


Type 'citation("pROC")' for a citation.



Attaching package: 'pROC'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    cov, smooth, var

library(ggplot2)

Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.4.3

library(mlbench)

Warning: package 'mlbench' was built under R version 4.4.3

title: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
text:
1.3 The Data
This guide uses three datasets to demonstrate ROC analysis across different applied contexts.

1.3.1 1. aSAH ÔÇö Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
Built into the pROC package. The dataset contains clinical and biomarker data from patients with subarachnoid hemorrhage. We use the biomarker s100b to predict a poor neurological outcome (outcome).

#load and preview dataset-asah

data(aSAH)
head(aSAH)

   gos6 outcome gender age wfns s100b  ndka
29    5    Good Female  42    1  0.13  3.01
30    5    Good Female  37    1  0.14  8.54
31    5    Good Female  42    1  0.10  8.09
32    5    Good Female  27    1  0.04 10.42
33    1    Poor Female  42    3  0.13 17.40
34    1    Poor   Male  48    2  0.10 12.75

str(aSAH)

'data.frame':   113 obs. of  7 variables:
 $ gos6   : Ord.factor w/ 5 levels "1"<"2"<"3"<"4"<..: 5 5 5 5 1 1 4 1 5 4 ...
 $ outcome: Factor w/ 2 levels "Good","Poor": 1 1 1 1 2 2 1 2 1 1 ...
 $ gender : Factor w/ 2 levels "Male","Female": 2 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 2 ...
 $ age    : int  42 37 42 27 42 48 57 41 49 75 ...
 $ wfns   : Ord.factor w/ 5 levels "1"<"2"<"3"<"4"<..: 1 1 1 1 3 2 5 4 1 2 ...
 $ s100b  : num  0.13 0.14 0.1 0.04 0.13 0.1 0.47 0.16 0.18 0.1 ...
 $ ndka   : num  3.01 8.54 8.09 10.42 17.4 ...




1.3.2 2. PimaIndiansDiabetes ÔÇö Diabetes Screening
From the mlbench package, the dataset contains health measurements for Pima Indian women. We use glucose to predict diabetes status (diabetes).

#load and preview data set-pima

data(PimaIndiansDiabetes)
head(PimaIndiansDiabetes)

  pregnant glucose pressure triceps insulin mass pedigree age diabetes
1        6     148       72      35       0 33.6    0.627  50      pos
2        1      85       66      29       0 26.6    0.351  31      neg
3        8     183       64       0       0 23.3    0.672  32      pos
4        1      89       66      23      94 28.1    0.167  21      neg
5        0     137       40      35     168 43.1    2.288  33      pos
6        5     116       74       0       0 25.6    0.201  30      neg

str(PimaIndiansDiabetes)

1.3.3 3. Cleveland Heart Disease Dataset
Loaded directly from the UCI Machine Learning Repository. We use thalach (maximum heart rate achieved) to predict heart disease presence (target).

#load-heart
heart <- read.csv(
  "https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/heart-disease/processed.cleveland.data",
  header = FALSE,
  na.strings = "?"
)
colnames(heart) <- c("age", "sex", "cp", "trestbps", "chol",
                     "fbs", "restecg", "thalach", "exang",
                     "oldpeak", "slope", "ca", "thal", "target")
heart$target <- ifelse(heart$target > 0, 1, 0)
head(heart)

6  56   1  2      120  236   0       0     178     0     0.8     1  0    3
  target

title: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
section: 1.4 Step 1: Is ROC Analysis Appropriate for Your Data?
text:
1.4 Step 1: Is ROC Analysis Appropriate for Your Data?
Before writing any code, confirm that ROC analysis is the right tool.
ROC analysis is appropriate when:

Your diagnostic test produces a continuous or ordinal result (e.g., a blood glucose level, a probability score)
You have a binary reference standard ÔÇö each observation is either diseased (1) or non-diseased (0)
Your goal is to evaluate overall discriminative ability across all possible thresholds, not performance at one specific cutoff

ROC analysis is not appropriate when:

Your test result is already binary (positive/negative)
Disease prevalence is very low
The costs of false positives and false negatives are very different ÔÇö ROC curves treat both error types equally


1.4.1 Quick Checklist



Question
Required Answer




Is the test result continuous or ordinal?
Yes


Is the outcome binary (diseased / not diseased)?
Yes


Am I interested in performance across all thresholds?
Yes


Is disease prevalence reasonably balanced?
Ideally yes
crumbs: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R

title: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
text:
1.5 Step 2: Prepare Your Data
Your dataset must have at minimum:

A numeric column for the test result
A binary column for the outcome (0/1 or a two-level factor)

Check your data structure and look for missing values before proceeding.

# Check structure
str(aSAH)

'data.frame':   113 obs. of  7 variables:
 $ gos6   : Ord.factor w/ 5 levels "1"<"2"<"3"<"4"<..: 5 5 5 5 1 1 4 1 5 4 ...
 $ outcome: Factor w/ 2 levels "Good","Poor": 1 1 1 1 2 2 1 2 1 1 ...
 $ gender : Factor w/ 2 levels "Male","Female": 2 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 2 ...
 $ age    : int  42 37 42 27 42 48 57 41 49 75 ...
 $ wfns   : Ord.factor w/ 5 levels "1"<"2"<"3"<"4"<..: 1 1 1 1 3 2 5 4 1 2 ...
 $ s100b  : num  0.13 0.14 0.1 0.04 0.13 0.1 0.47 0.16 0.18 0.1 ...
 $ ndka   : num  3.01 8.54 8.09 10.42 17.4 ...

# Check for missing values
sum(is.na(aSAH$s100b))

[1] 0

sum(is.na(aSAH$outcome))

[1] 0

# Confirm outcome levels
table(aSAH$outcome)


Good Poor 
  72   41 


The pROC package drops missing values automatically, but you should know how many observations are affected. If missingness is substantial or non-random, address it before running the analysis.
crumbs: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R

title: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
text:
1.6 Step 3: Generate the ROC Curve
Use pROC::roc() to fit the ROC curve. The two key arguments are:

response: the binary outcome variable
predictor: the continuous test result


roc_asah <- roc(response = aSAH$outcome, predictor = aSAH$s100b)

Setting levels: control = Good, case = Poor


Setting direction: controls < cases

roc_asah


Call:
roc.default(response = aSAH$outcome, predictor = aSAH$s100b)

Data: aSAH$s100b in 72 controls (aSAH$outcome Good) < 41 cases (aSAH$outcome Poor).
Area under the curve: 0.7314



1.6.1 Understanding the Direction Warning
pROC will print a message about the direction it assumed (e.g., direction: controls < cases). This tells you whether higher test values are assumed to indicate disease. Do not ignore this. If the assumed direction is wrong for your test, your curve will be mirrored and your AUC will be below 0.5. You can set the direction explicitly:

# Higher s100b -> worse outcome: controls < cases (default is correct here)
roc_asah <- roc(response = aSAH$outcome,
                predictor = aSAH$s100b,
                direction = "<")

Setting levels: control = Good, case = Poor
crumbs: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R

title: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
text:
1.7 Step 4: Compute and Interpret AUC
The Area Under the Curve (AUC) summarizes the ROC curve in a single number.

AUC = 0.5: the test performs no better than random chance
AUC = 1.0: the test perfectly separates diseased from non-diseased individuals
AUC = 0.7ÔÇô0.8: generally considered acceptable discrimination
AUC > 0.8: generally considered excellent discrimination

These are rough benchmarks ÔÇö what counts as ÔÇťgood enoughÔÇŁ depends on your clinical or scientific context.

auc(roc_asah)

Area under the curve: 0.7314

ci.auc(roc_asah)

95% CI: 0.6301-0.8326 (DeLong)


Repeat for the other datasets:

roc_pima <- roc(response = PimaIndiansDiabetes$diabetes,
                predictor = PimaIndiansDiabetes$glucose,
                direction = "<")

Setting levels: control = neg, case = pos

auc(roc_pima)

Area under the curve: 0.7881

ci.auc(roc_pima)

95% CI: 0.7546-0.8217 (DeLong)



heart_clean <- na.omit(heart[, c("thalach", "target")])
roc_heart <- roc(response = heart_clean$target,
                 predictor = heart_clean$thalach,
                 direction = ">")  # higher heart rate -> less likely disease

Setting levels: control = 0, case = 1

auc(roc_heart)

Area under the curve: 0.7453

ci.auc(roc_heart)

95% CI: 0.6901-0.8005 (DeLong)
crumbs: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R

title: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
text:
1.8 Step 5: Visualize the ROC Curve
A good visualization shows the curve, the diagonal reference line (random chance), and the AUC value.

1.8.1 Basic Plot with pROC

plot(roc_asah,
     main = "ROC Curve: s100b predicting poor outcome (aSAH)",
     col = "#2c7bb6",
     lwd = 2)
legend("bottomright",
       legend = paste("AUC =", round(auc(roc_asah), 3)),
       col = "#2c7bb6", lwd = 2)










1.8.2 Polished Plot with ggplot2

roc_df <- data.frame(
  specificity = roc_asah$specificities,
  sensitivity = roc_asah$sensitivities
)

ggplot(roc_df, aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2c7bb6", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.75, y = 0.1,
           label = paste("AUC =", round(auc(roc_asah), 3)),
           size = 5, color = "#2c7bb6") +
  labs(title = "ROC Curve: s100b predicting poor outcome (aSAH)",
       x = "1 - Specificity (False Positive Rate)",
       y = "Sensitivity (True Positive Rate)") +
  theme_minimal()










1.8.3 Comparing Multiple ROC Curves

ggroc(list(aSAH = roc_asah, Diabetes = roc_pima, Heart = roc_heart),
      linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  labs(title = "ROC Curve Comparison Across Datasets",
       x = "1 - Specificity", y = "Sensitivity",
       color = "Dataset") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R

title: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
text:
1.9 Step 6: Evaluate Your Results
Once you have a curve and AUC, ask yourself:

Does the curve bow toward the upper-left corner? If it hugs the diagonal, the test has little discriminative ability.
Is the AUC above 0.5? If not, check whether the direction argument is set correctly.
Does the observation count in the pROC output match your dataset? If not, check for unexpected missing values.
Is the confidence interval for the AUC reasonably narrow? Wide intervals suggest insufficient sample size.
Does the AUC make sense given what is known about this test from prior literature?
crumbs: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R

title: 1┬á Evaluating Diagnostic Tests with ROC Curves in R
text:
1.10 Additional Resources

pROC vignette: Run vignette("pROC") in R or see Robin et al. (2011) for the full paper
Fawcett (2006): A readable conceptual introduction to ROC analysis (Fawcett 2006)
Pepe (2003): A comprehensive textbook treatment of diagnostic test evaluation (pepe2003?)





Fawcett, Tom. 2006. ÔÇťAn Introduction to ROC Analysis.ÔÇŁ Pattern Recognition Letters 27 (8): 861ÔÇô74. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.patrec.2005.10.010.


Robin, Xavier, Natacha Turck, Alexandre Hainard, Natalia Tiberti, Fr├ęd├ęrique Lisacek, Jean-Charles Sanchez, and Markus M├╝ller. 2011. ÔÇťpROC: An Open-Source Package for R and S+ to Analyze and Compare ROC Curves.ÔÇŁ BMC Bioinformatics 12 (1): 77. https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2105-12-77.

title: Appendix A ÔÇö Needs Assessment
text:
What does someone trying to accomplish your chosen task need help with?
They would need the most help with the implementation of coding. Assuming this guide is intended for applied researchers in biology and medicine. Users new to using these packages or coding will likely struggle a bit to connect the underlying concepts to actual implementation in R.
What parts are likely to be tricky?
The trickiest part for most users is choosing the right R package and functions. Several options exist (pROC, ROCR, OptimalCutpoints), and it is not always obvious which to use or how the key functions relate to one another. Users may also be unsure when ROC analysis is actually appropriate versus when it might be misleading. Might also add some requirements for the data that is being used.
What resources are already available on this topic that may be helpful? Look for e.g.┬ásoftware vignettes, package documentation, papers about software packages, and so on.
Some that I found were: pROC package vignette Robin et al.┬á(2011) paper in BMC Bioinformatics Fawcett (2006) Pattern Recognition Letters
(will use these to help describe in more depth what the curves purposes are and how to draw conclusions that are statistically correct based on the produced results.)

B Needs Assessment

title: Appendix B ÔÇö Task Analysis
text:
B.1 Additional Guidance
Here are some questions to guide you through the process of doing a task analysis. You donÔÇÖt have to specifically answer each one of these (and some may not apply), but they should get you started thinking in the right direction.
What are the prerequisites, for both knowledge and e.g.┬ásoftware setup?
Users should understand basic diagnostic testing concepts: sensitivity, specificity, and binary disease outcomes. Basic R skills are required loading data, installing packages, and running functions. Users will need R, RStudio, and the pROC and ggplot2 packages installed.
What are the basic requirements for any data that the method is used on?
What are the basic components of the task? - Outline these in a bit more detail
What decisions does the user need to be prepared to make on the fly?
What questions should the user ask of the ÔÇťfirst draftÔÇŁ of the product? What adjustments may need to be made?
adjustments
-If the AUC is below 0.5, the test direction is likely flipped and might require user to reverse the direction argument.
Your check-in should answer these basic questions (and similar concerns that apply more directly to your topic).
Once youÔÇÖve completed the check-in, you can use this section to jump-start an introduction/set-up/getting started section in your user guide. This document should remain as an appendix to your main report.
